data.title: ‘F1: The Movie’ speeds to $55.6M debut, dominates U.S. box office
data.description:
Warner Bros.’ (NASDAQ:WBD) much-anticipated F1: The Movie raced to the top of the North American box office this weekend, delivering a $55.6 million opening, according to studio estimates compiled by Comscore. The racing drama roared ahead of the competition, marking one of the strongest debuts for an original sports film in recent years.

Universal’s (NASDAQ:CMCSA) How to Train Your Dragon held the No. 2 spot with $19.4 million in its third weekend, pushing its domestic total past $200 million. Meanwhile, Disney’s (NYSE:DIS) Elio took third place with $10.7 million, despite a steep 49% decline from its debut frame.

Universal’s (NASDAQ:CMCSA) M3GAN 2.0, the latest installment in the AI horror franchise, launched with $10.2 million, slightly trailing expectations. Sony’s (NYSE:SONY) 28 Years Later, the sequel to the apocalyptic hit, rounded out the top five with $9.7 million, down 68% in its second week.

Disney’s (NYSE:DIS) Lilo & Stitch live-action remake added $6.9 million for a strong $400 million domestic haul after six weeks. Paramount’s (NASDAQ:PARA) Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning continues to show staying power with $4.15 million in its sixth week.

Among indie and specialty releases, A24’s Materialists brought in just under $3 million, Lionsgate’s (NYSE:LION) Ballerina earned $2.13 million, and Sony’s (NYSE:SONY) Karate Kid: Legends added $1 million despite a major theater count drop.

Overall, the top 10 films generated $122.8 million in domestic (United States and Canada) ticket sales.

Top 10 Movies — Domestic Box Office (June 27–29, 2025):

  1. F1: The Movie (Warner Bros.) — $55,600,000
  2. How to Train Your Dragon (Universal) — $19,400,000 (Cume: $200,057,000)
  3. Elio (Disney) — $10,700,000 (Cume: $42,225,747)
  4. M3GAN 2.0 (Universal) — $10,200,000
  5. 28 Years Later (Sony) — $9,700,000 (Cume: $50,354,000)
  6. Lilo & Stitch (Disney) — $6,900,000 (Cume: $400,070,904)
  7. Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning (Paramount) — $4,150,000 (Cume: $185,996,000)
  8. Materialists (A24) — $2,999,422 (Cume: $30,401,381)
  9. Ballerina (Lionsgate) — $2,130,000 (Cume: $55,473,000)
  10. Karate Kid: Legends (Sony) — $1,000,000 (Cume: $51,592,000)

data.title: Warehouse automation investment set to rise, machine vision in focus: UBS
data.description:
Global spending on warehouse automation is poised to accelerate, driven by technological advances and rising labor costs, according to new survey data from UBS Evidence Lab. The findings highlight strong secular tailwinds for automation equipment providers, with machine vision specialist Cognex (NASDAQ:CGNX) emerging as a key beneficiary.

The sixth wave of UBS's Global Warehouse Automation Business Survey, which polled 130 decision-makers across North America, Europe and Asia-Pacific, found that nearly two-irds of respondents plan to increase automation capital expenditures at warehouses and distribution centers over the next year, with an average budget increase of 5%. Retailers and e-commerce companies are leading near-term spending intentions, but wholesalers and manufacturers appear even more bullish over the next three years.

UBS continues to see structural growth in warehouse automation, with limited market saturation to date, analyst Damian Karas wrote in a June 26 research note, adding that a wave of automation investment will unfold through the end of the decade.

Machine vision demand accelerates

The survey indicates growing momentum for advanced machine vision technologies, critical for automating barcode reading, quality inspection, and item recognition in logistics environments. More than half, or 56%, of respondents expect to boost spending on machine vision tools in the next year, marking an uptick from prior survey waves.

While 62% of respondents have implemented some form of image-based barcode scanning, only 36% of their warehouse space utilizes advanced machine vision technology, signaling significant room for expansion.

Cognex (NASDAQ:CGNX), a leader in the space, ranked as the top vendor under consideration for future deployments, surpassing peers such as Japan’s Keyence and China's Hikvision. Datalogic and Zebra Technologies (NASDAQ:ZBRA) remain the most widely used machine vision and barcode scanning suppliers, with Zebra (NASDAQ:ZBRA) also maintaining strong brand positioning for mobile robotics.

Mobile robots and AMRs gain

Mobile robots, particularly Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs), are seeing rapid adoption as companies seek flexible automation solutions that avoid costly fixed infrastructure. An overwhelming 91% of survey participants already use or plan to use mobile robots, reflecting a notable increase from previous survey rounds.

Among AMR providers, Honeywell’s (NASDAQ:HON) Intelligrated business scored in the top tier for customer consideration, with Zebra's (ZBRA) Fetch Robotics positioned mid-pack. European automation players continue to dominate market penetration, although Intelligrated's improving standing suggests growing competitiveness among U.S. vendors.

The rising use of AMRs is a function of their flexibility, Karas said, adding that businesses want automation that doesn’t require being bolted to the floor.

Stock Takeaways: Positive for CGNX, mixed for HON, SYM

UBS views the survey as broadly supportive of warehouse automation equities, particularly Cognex, given its leading position in machine vision supplier consideration and the relatively low ranking of Chinese rival Hikvision.

Zebra (ZBRA) continues to show brand strength across both barcode scanning and mobile robotics, while Symbotic (NASDAQ:SYM) appears to face headwinds with the weakest standing among potential new suppliers. Honeywell's (NASDAQ:HON) performance was mixed: the company's utilization slipped versus past surveys, but future customer intentions for its automation and AMR offerings improved.

While Honeywell (HON) is seeing encouraging signs in warehouse automation, the segment remains a relatively small piece of the broader Honeywell narrative, Karas said.

Honeywell (HON) this year announced a major restructuring plan to split into three independent, publicly traded companies: automation, aerospace and advanced materials. The move, set to be completed by mid-2026 is set to streamline operations and sharpen each business’s strategic focus.

Wave of automation still to come

Despite increased spending intentions, UBS said industry-wide automation adoption remains limited, leaving ample runway for growth. The firm expects rising labor costs, AI-powered optimization and e-commerce logistics demands to continue driving investment through 2030.

The survey also highlighted growing interest in AI-based tools for warehouse layout optimization and digital twin technologies, with Intelligrated scoring favorably among respondents evaluating those advanced solutions.

Overall, UBS maintains a constructive view on the warehouse automation sector, particularly for companies like Cognex (CGNX) and Zebra (ZBRA) that are well-positioned in high-growth subsegments such as machine vision and mobile robotics.

The UBS Evidence Lab survey, conducted between April 17 and May 20, 2025, gathered responses from warehouse automation decision-makers across EMEA (31%), Asia-Pacific (38%) and North America (31%). The report marks the sixth wave of the study on global automation trends.

data.title: Weekly top consumer staples gainers and losers: Estée Lauder Companies lead, Bunge Global ends in red
data.description:
Wall Street ended higher last week, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite hitting record closing highs amid trade negotiations. Sentiment was buoyed by optimism surrounding U.S. trade progress with China and other partners and the stable Middle East ceasefire, easing broader trade conflict concerns.

The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLP) for the week, which holds a 6.31% weightage on the S&P 500 ended flat, where the S&P 500's (SP500) ended 3.06% higher. However, the sector's year-to-date performance is considerably higher, +2.47%, which is notably below the benchmark S&P 500's (SP500) 4.96%.

Top gainers this week:

The Estée Lauder Companies (EL) +6.5% after HSBC upgraded the stock to a Buy rating after having it set at Hold. Importantly, the magnitude of the company's restructuring program and recent USD weakness is seen signalling an end to the earnings downgrade cycle.

McCormick & Co. (MKC) +4.5% as it sees itself as well positioned with robust plans to mitigate current tariff related costs, fuel growth investments, and expand operating margins.

Target (TGT) +3.9% as it eyes shipping products straight from its factories.

Brown-Forman (BF.B) ended the week 3.0% higher.

Dollar General (DG) ended the week 2.6% higher as it joins the long list of retailers with special July sales events.

Top losers this week:

Bunge Global (BG) is the top loser for the week, with stock price ended 5.8% lower.

Conagra Brands (CAG) -5.1% as its facing cost pressures for chicken, beef and pork exacerbated by limited pricing power, which faces a “meaningful” headwind to earnings growth along with increased margin pressures, leading BofA Securities to downgrade the packaged food producer to Underperform from Neutral, and shave 26% off.

General Mills (GIS) stock ended 4.7% lower as RBC Capital Markets analyst, Nik Modi acknowledged that investor sentiment around packaged food remains poor and noted that fundamentals have yet to perform.

The Campbell's Co. (CPB) -3.3% on gloomy outlook and the Co.’s own conservative profit outlook for FY25, shares set a new low on Friday after a three-day losing streak.

Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) ended the week 3.0% lower.

data.title: SA Roundtable: Will the U.S. adopt drug price controls?
data.description:
The high cost of prescription drugs in America has again moved back into the political spotlight, with President Trump recently signing an executive order aimed at bringing prices more in line with those in other industrialized nations.

In a May survey of Seeking Alpha readers, 56.2% of 1,187 respondents said the U.S. government should adopt price controls, while 43.8% disagreed. When asked if they thought Trump would prevail in lowering prices, 52.4% of 1,190 respondents thought he would, while 47.6% thought he wouldn't. 

But is the United States ready to embrace drug price controls? If the pricing process is reformed, what would it look like? Would there still be a place for big pharmacy benefit managers, or PBMs, like CVS Health (NYSE:CVS), Cigna (NYSE:CI) and UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH)?

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Stephen Ayers and Edmund Ingham what they thought drug pricing reform might look like in the U.S. 

Do you think the U.S. will adopt price controls on prescription drugs?

Stephen Ayers: There is a near-universal consensus on the need to reduce drug costs. I believe things are pointing to drug price controls. However, it will likely be a long process. The issue is that there is no consensus on how to reduce drug costs. The Inflation Reduction Act, or IRA, signed by President Biden in 2022, was a good starting point. This authorized the federal government to negotiate prices that Medicare pays for selected medicines.

Edmund Ingham: President Trump is desperate to put right the problem of U.S. citizens paying much more for their drugs than anywhere else in the world, and wants to bypass Pharmacy Benefit Managers, the middlemen who negotiate with insurers and drug companies to establish prices for U.S. drugs. PBMs and drug companies have exceptionally powerful lobbies, however, and President Trump failed to achieve the same goal during his first administration.

As such, I would say the jury remains out on whether the President will be able to introduce price controls on drugs. The issues are more complex than Americans simply paying far too much for their drugs. The Trump administration has a limited time in power, a problem drug companies do not have.

If the U.S. does adopt price controls, what do you think they would look like?

Stephen Ayers: I believe drug price controls will involve a combination of existing and new mechanisms. We are already seeing whispers of IRA expansion. For instance, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services, or CMS, is already planning to include Medicare Part B drugs in negotiations starting in 2028. Part B drugs are typically not self-administered (e.g., chemotherapy drugs, immunotherapy, vaccines, durable medical equipment). So, this is an expansion of the government's hand. 

As for new mechanisms, President Trump has proposed a "Most Favored Nation" model that aims to ensure that Americans pay no more for drugs than in other developed countries. However, this faces many challenges, including legal complexities. 

Finally, Value-Based Pricing, or VBP, is a model that provides an optimal cost of a drug based on the value it delivers to the healthcare system. However, VBP comes with its own set of hurdles, such as measuring real-world performance. In my view, the best solution is likely the one that combines multiple methods.

Edmund Ingham: If President Trump gets his wish, Americans would be allowed to request “Most Favored Nation” pricing, i.e. the drug company would have to sell its drug at the lowest price it sells it in any other country and no higher. 

Research confirms that Americans, on average, pay at least two to three times more for brand name drugs than consumers in other countries, but it also suggests that they pay less for generic drugs, which account for ~90% of all filled prescriptions. Ironically, Trump’s tariffs may result in generic drug prices increasing, as they are mostly manufactured overseas. 

I think what we will see is a continuation of what is already happening - the U.S. government selecting some of the biggest selling brand-name drugs, such as Bristol Myers (BMY) and Pfizer's (PFE) Eliquis, Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Wegovy and Bristol Myers' (BMY) Revlimid, and negotiating to force those prices down. I do believe prices for some of the biggest selling drugs will continue to fall. Price hiking is a problem within pharmaceutical markets.

In May, President Trump called for eliminating PBMs from the drug pricing process. Do you think PBMs will be excluded? If so, what would happen to them?

Stephen Ayers: Both sides of the aisle agree that PBM reform is necessary. However, PBMs are deeply embedded in the U.S. healthcare system, and uprooting the whole thing could "harm the lawn" as well. I do not believe any administration is likely to eliminate PBMs. Instead, legislators should push PBMs to be more transparent and accountable. For example, reforms should target popular PBM practices like "spread pricing" and "rebate retention," both of which are revenue-generating strategies that likely contribute to the rising cost of drugs in the U.S.

Edmund Ingham: In my view, a four-year administration is simply not enough time to eliminate PBMs from the drug pricing process. We know that UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) and CVS (NYSE:CVS), who run two of the largest PBMs, are struggling and facing demands for change, but let’s balance that against the fact shareholders have agreed to pay the incoming UnitedHealth CEO $1 million per annum plus a $60 million equity award. It’s hard to believe that there is genuine commitment among those at the top of the industry to drive real change - the best that can be hoped for is concessions and a more transparent PBM industry, not its elimination. I don’t see that happening.

  • Top Pharmaceuticals Stocks
  • Top Biotechnology Stocks

data.title: Top weekly S&P 500 consumer discretionary gainers & losers: Nike takes lead; eBay bottoms
data.description:
For the week ending June 27, the S&P 500 Consumer Discretionary sector (NYSEARCA:XLY) rose 1.95%, underperforming the broader S&P 500's (SP500) 2.46% gain. Year-to-date, the sector is down 2.57%, significantly trailing the benchmark's 4.96% rise.

Despite this, consumer discretionary stocks saw some uplift this week. Sentiment was buoyed by optimism surrounding U.S. trade progress with China and other partners (including a signed truce and flexible tariff deadlines) and the stable Middle East ceasefire, easing broader trade conflict concerns.

Top S&P 500 consumer discretionary gainers:

Nike (NYSE:NKE) +20.49% Shares rose after Nike's FQ4 results exceeded low expectations, supported by promotional pricing on sneakers. Management noted the results still fell short of goals but anticipated a smaller Q1 revenue decline. Nike also warned of a $1B cost impact from U. S. tariffs.

Carnival Corporation (CCL) +14.68% Shares rose after the cruise operator's strong Q2 results, increasing guidance for net income by 40% and adjusted EBITDA to $6.9B, with CEO noting early achievement of sustainability targets.

Royal Caribbean Cruises (NYSE:RCL) +13.63% 

DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) +9.91% Shares reached a record high after analyst Josh Beck upgraded the stock to Strong Buy, citing its “underappreciated” valuation linked to the Deliveroo acquisition, projecting notable EBITDA growth.

Booking.com (NASDAQ:BKNG) +7.34% In a report, two Dutch consumer organizations are enlisting affected customers for a legal action against Booking.com. The Consumer Competition Claims Foundation (CCC) and the Consumers' Association said in a statement that it has overcharged for hotel prices since 2013, resulting in hundreds of millions of euros in harm to Dutch consumers.

Top S&P 500 consumer discretionary losers:

eBay (EBAY) -4.97%

Darden Restaurants (DRI) -4.27%

Ulta Beauty (ULTA) -3.29% The company announced Chris Lialios as interim CFO immediately. The company confirmed its fiscal 2025 guidance, projecting comparable store sales growth of 0% to 1.5% and diluted EPS between $22.65 and $23.20.

CarMax’s (NYSE:KMX) -2.87% CarMax reported better-than-expected first quarter results, despite initial share gains, concerns about the economy and loan delinquencies impacted long-term confidence. Analyst Rajat Gupta noted the potential for higher P/E multiples, though future visibility is uncertain.

Tractor Supply (NASDAQ:TSCO) -1.54%

Notable Consumer Discretionary ETFs include: (NYSEARCA:XLY), (NYSEARCA:VCR), (NYSEARCA:FXD), (NYSEARCA:FDIS), (NYSEARCA:RSPD), and (NYSEARCA:RXI).

data.title: Meta is said to recruit more AI talent from OpenAI
data.description:
Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) is continuing its hiring spree of artificial intelligence experts from OpenAI, with four more researchers set to join the company, according to a report from The Information on Saturday.

The new recruits -- Shengjia Zhao, Jiahui Yu, Shuchao Bi and Hongyu Ren -- have each agreed to join Meta, the report said, citing a source familiar with the matter.

This follows news earlier in the week that Meta (NASDAQ:META) had hired Lucas Beyer, Alexander Kolesnikov and Xiaohua Zhai, all of whom previously worked in OpenAI’s Zurich office, according to the Wall Street Journal.

Both Meta (META) and OpenAI, the developer behind ChatGPT, declined to comment when contacted by Reuters.

Meta (META) has recently accelerated its efforts to attract AI talent from OpenAI, as Chief Executive Mark Zuckerberg expands the company's work in advanced artificial intelligence and what's known as superintelligence research.

data.title: Elon Musk slams Senate tax and spending bill, warns of economic fallout
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) Chief Executive Elon Musk sharply criticized the latest version of President Donald Trump's tax and spending proposal, calling it "utterly insane and destructive" just weeks after the two appeared to reconcile following earlier disagreements over the legislation.

Musk, who previously clashed with Trump over the bill's contents, took to social platform X on Saturday to voice his concerns. "This Senate bill will destroy millions of American jobs and inflict significant long-term harm on the country," Musk posted.

The billionaire entrepreneur argued that the proposed legislation favors outdated industries while undermining sectors critical to the future of U.S. competitiveness, particularly technology and clean energy.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump says group of wealthy investors ready to acquire TikTok
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump announced during a television interview on Sunday that a buyer has been identified for TikTok, consisting of what he described as "very wealthy people," though he said their identities will be revealed in approximately two weeks.

Trump shared the update during an appearance on Fox News Channel's "Sunday Morning Futures with Maria Bartiromo."

The social-video platform, which has been under mounting pressure to divest from Chinese ownership, faced a deadline earlier this year to secure a sale to a non-Chinese buyer or risk being banned in the United States.

data.title: Traders are said to shift hedges as Mideast tensions ease
data.description:
U.S. stocks reached fresh highs last week and oil prices pulled back as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East appeared to subside. Yet, beneath the surface of options markets, signs of caution persist as investors hedge against potential future shocks tied to global events or economic uncertainty, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

In the short term, investor sentiment around equities is turning more optimistic. The cost of protective put options relative to calls has narrowed, reflecting reduced demand for downside protection. However, sentiment further out on the horizon remains cautious, with longer-term pricing dynamics largely unchanged. This includes elevated prices for longer-dated Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) futures, pointing to ongoing concerns about issues like tariffs and broader market stability.

The derivatives landscape hasn’t fully stabilized to where it was in February, said Rocky Fishman, founder of research firm Asym 500, in a report Friday. He said the VIX futures curve has steepened, with mid-range contracts around six months out staying at levels rarely seen in 2023 and 2024.

More bullish on near-term equities

Shrinking put premiums suggest confidence, but long-term positioning remains cautious.

Last week also brought signs of increased demand for VIX call options, contracts that typically profit from a spike in volatility. The demand was seen even as the Cboe VVIX Index, which measures volatility of volatility, slipped below 90, marking its lowest reading since July of last year.

In the oil market, volatility has receded following the Israel-Iran conflict, but caution remains. Brent crude’s (CO1:COM) implied volatility has returned to early June levels, with option pricing showing little directional bias. However, option premiums for oil swings remain elevated compared to equities, especially after the S&P 500’s (SP500) record-setting rally placed downward pressure on stock market volatility.

Traders eye oil-equity correlations

J.P. Morgan Chase (JPM) derivatives strategists, including Emma Wu, recently recommended hybrid trades linking oil and equities, Bloomberg News reported. They suggested that renewed geopolitical instability could push oil prices higher, while prolonged elevated interest rates could pressure stocks lower. Although oil and equities have shown increased positive correlation recently, the two tend to decouple during major geopolitical disruptions.

The recent rapid shifts in global risk have left many investors on edge. Data from ICE Futures Europe shows hedge funds and other large investors scaled back bullish bets on Brent crude at the fastest pace since April during the week ending June 24. This followed a period of building long positions, which had reached an 11-week high.

Meanwhile, trend-following Commodity Trading Advisors (CTAs) quickly reversed course in the European natural gas market. By last Tuesday, these funds shifted to an 18% net short position from 9% net long, according to Bridgeton Research Group data cited by Bloomberg News. Such algorithm-driven strategies often amplify price moves, complicating risk management for traders with exposure to physical commodities.

Crude oil calendar spreads attract heavy interest

One corner of the oil market seeing an unusual spike in activity is calendar spread options: contracts that allow traders to bet on the price difference between near-term and longer-dated oil futures. Open interest in these contracts has surged to record highs this month.

Traders appear to be wagering that short-term tightness in oil supply could give way to a surplus later in the year, as OPEC and other producers boost output while uncertainty around global demand persists. That curve structure, likened to a “hockey stick,” briefly disappeared during the height of the Israel-Iran conflict but has since reverted to its pre-crisis formation, Bloomberg News reported.

Geopolitical risk premium shrinks, but caution persists

Goldman Sachs analysts, including Yulia Zhestkova Grigsby and Daan Struyven, suggested in a recent note that the easing of geopolitical risk premiums reflects market expectations shaped by recent experience.

Major geopolitical flare-ups have occurred without severe disruptions to oil supplies, while both the U.S. and China appear motivated to avoid escalating tensions. They also pointed to expectations of significant inventory builds heading into the fall, further pressuring oil prices, Bloomberg News reported.

data.title: M&A activity rebounds amid market uncertainty, Trump’s trade policies
data.description:
Corporate dealmaking in the United States is off to its strongest first-half performance since 2022, signaling that appetite for mergers and acquisitions remains resilient in the face of market volatility, global conflicts and President Trump’s shifting tariff landscape, The Wall Street Journal reported on Sunday.

According to data from the London Stock Exchange Group cited by the newspaper, the total value of U.S. deals through June 25 has climbed roughly 10% compared to the same period last year, reaching its highest level in three years.

Deal activity stumbled early in the second quarter after Trump’s broad “Liberation Day” tariffs, introduced in April, rattled markets and temporarily dampened enthusiasm among dealmakers. However, activity has since regained momentum.

Several significant transactions have taken place in sectors less affected by Trump’s trade measures. Notable among them, Charter Communications (CHTR) agreed to acquire Cox Communications for nearly $22 billion, consolidating the broadband and cable market. In the tech sector, Salesforce (CRM) revived its approximately $8 billion acquisition of Informatica, underscoring the industry's push to strengthen AI capabilities.

Footwear brand Skechers also made headlines with a deal to take the company private, valued at more than $9 billion.

Despite the uptick in total deal value, the overall number of transactions has fallen 16% this year, a decline driven largely by fewer deals under $1 billion, which traditionally make up the bulk of market activity.

Advisers say some acquirers are holding back, waiting for more economic clarity before pursuing major transactions. Wall Street still anticipates a robust M&A cycle under Trump’s administration, though the timing may extend beyond initial expectations.

Private equity has continued to play an outsized role in the market. LSEG reports that private-equity-backed deals are up nearly 21% in value so far this year, although the number of transactions has dropped by 20%.

The conditions are aligning for stronger dealmaking in the second half of the year, particularly if inflation cools further and the Federal Reserve moves to lower interest rates, the Journal reported. Capital remains available, and private equity firms are under pressure to deliver returns to investors while putting their raised funds to work.

The market for new stock offerings is also showing signs of revival. Major U.S. indexes have rebounded following the initial turbulence from Trump’s tariffs.

Recent IPOs have added to the optimism. Virtual physical therapy provider Hinge Health (HNGE) saw its shares jump 17% in its market debut in late May. Two weeks later, Circle Internet Group, a cryptocurrency company, delivered one of the most talked-about listings in recent memory.

In another encouraging sign, companies that have gone public in the past six weeks have, on average, outperformed the broader market, suggesting investor appetite for new offerings is strengthening.
